nativeRange.habitat: Shaded marshy riverbanks in south-western India (Karnataka and Kerala states), at elevations up to roughly 1,200 m. Grows along slow streams and seepage areas where its creeping rhizome anchors in soft, nutrient-rich substrate.

decorPreferences: moderate to bright lighting for color expression
rhizome planted on or just above the substrate (do not bury the rhizome)

avoidWith: Silver Dollars and other plant-eating fish

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Krzysztof Ziarnek (Kenraiz), taken 2022-07-05 at Botanischer Garten Halle. 2711x3613 px, 1.73 MB. Clear specimen shot showing characteristic lance-shaped leaves and creeping rhizome; suitable as primary image with attribution.
Commercial product photo of the 'Red' trade variant showing the red-violet leaf coloration. Watermark/branding risk; use only as visual reference, not as primary image.
Secondary product shot of the 'Red' trade variant. Commercial use restricted; reference only.

summary: A slow-growing rosette plant from south-western India and a sister genus to Cryptocoryne. Known for lance-shaped leaves with red-purple undersides and trade variants including 'Red', 'Pink', 'Green', and 'Toxic Yellow'. Beginner-friendly but prone to acclimation melt.
careNotes: Lagenandra meeboldii is a slow-growing rosette plant native to shaded marshy riverbanks in south-western India (Karnataka and Kerala states), at elevations up to roughly 1,200 m. The genus Lagenandra is the sister genus to Cryptocoryne (both in family Araceae) and is endemic to the Indian subcontinent (India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh); the two genera are distinguished botanically by leaf vernation — Lagenandra exhibits involute vernation while Cryptocoryne is convolute. Visually, Lagenandra meeboldii is recognizable by its distinctive pointed, lance-shaped leaves with red-purple undersides; the leaf blade is typically 6-12 cm long and 4-8 cm wide (maximum ~15 cm), and mature plants reach 10-20 cm tall under typical aquarium conditions, occasionally up to 30 cm under good light and nutrients, with a rosette width of 15-30 cm. Several trade variants exist: 'Red' is the most common in the hobby (dusty green base with violet to red-violet flush, new leaves emerging pale pink), with 'Pink', 'Green', and the rare 'Toxic Yellow' also encountered. Lighting is flexible — Buce Plant lists low-to-medium light as acceptable, while Aquasabi recommends at least moderate light to develop the leaf coloration; brighter light intensifies the reds and violets without being strictly required. CO2 is NOT required, but the plant is reported to respond well to CO2 injection with somewhat faster growth. Lagenandra meeboldii is a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER — nutrient uptake happens primarily through the root zone via a creeping rhizome roughly 8 mm thick, so a nutrient-rich substrate (aqua soil) and/or regular root tabs near the root mass are strongly recommended; liquid water-column fertilization is secondary. IMPORTANT PLANTING NOTE: like Anubias and Bucephalandra, the rhizome must NOT be buried — plant by anchoring the roots in substrate with the rhizome sitting on or just above the substrate surface, or tied to hardscape; burying the rhizome will cause it to rot. CRYPT-STYLE MELT WARNING: like its sister-genus Cryptocoryne, Lagenandra meeboldii frequently sheds most or all of its existing leaves during acclimation or after a major water-parameter change. This is normal — new leaves adapted to the tank conditions will regrow within 2-4 weeks; do not remove the rhizome and roots during a melt event. Growth rate is slow even after acclimation (Aquasabi: slow; Buce Plant: moderate), which makes Lagenandra meeboldii an excellent low-maintenance midground specimen plant. Temperature consensus is 22-28 °C for the planted-tank sweet spot, with Aquasabi reporting a wider 12-28 °C tolerance. pH 6.0-7.5 is the practical aquarium range and the plant accepts a wide gH/kH range (gH 0-30 dGH, kH 0-14 dKH per Aquasabi). The plant can be grown both submerged and emersed. Source disagreement note: Buce Plant lists growth rate as 'moderate' while Aquasabi lists 'slow'; this entry uses 'slow' as the more conservative aquarium-realistic value and as a closer match to the user-supplied research note describing the species as a slow grower.
breedingNotes: Propagated by cutting the creeping rhizome into pieces, each retaining leaves and roots, or by removing developing side shoots once they have their own root system. Lagenandra meeboldii does NOT spread by underground runners (unlike Cryptocoryne wendtii). The plant can flower (producing the spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of Araceae) when grown emersed, but seed propagation is not practical in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Aquasabi
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temperature 12-28 °C with 22-28 °C optimal, pH 6-7, gH 0-30 dGH, kH 0-14 dKH), height (10-20 cm typical, up to 30 cm), width (15-30 cm), leaf dimensions (6-12 x 4-8 cm), light demand (at least moderate), CO2 demand (not required but appreciated), growth rate (slow), rhizome thickness (~8 mm), native range (south-western India, Karnataka and Kerala, up to 1200 m), propagation method (cut rhizome or side shoots), difficulty (easy), and the note that the plant is not an underground-runner species.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (India), pH range (6-7.5, used as the upper bound), low-to-medium light tolerance, CO2 not necessary, midground placement, propagation by rhizome cutting, easy difficulty, and the 'Red' cultivar coloration (dusty green with violet/red-violet hues, pale-pink new leaves). NOTE: Buce Plant lists growth rate as 'moderate' which conflicts with Aquasabi's 'slow' and with the user-supplied note; this entry uses 'slow'.

additional.name: Tropica
additional.notes: Confirmed easy difficulty and rosulate (rosette) form. Notes that good light conditions enhance development of leaf colors. Less parameter detail than Aquasabi; used as a tertiary confirmation source.

additional.name: Wikipedia (genus page)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales), confirmation that the genus is endemic to the Indian subcontinent (India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh), and the involute-vs-convolute vernation distinction from sister genus Cryptocoryne. Confirms Lagenandra meeboldii is distributed in south-western India (matches Aquasabi). The species-specific Wikipedia article (Lagenandra_meeboldii) returned HTTP 404 at access time.

additional.name: Buce Plant (search index)
additional.notes: Cross-checked trade-variant availability: 'Red' (most common, also offered as UNS tissue culture) and 'Green' (UNS tissue culture) are currently in commercial supply. 'Pink' and 'Toxic Yellow' were noted in the research brief but were not present in Buce Plant's current listings; they may be rarer or seasonal.
